I agree with agent1’s conclusion that congenital hyperthyroidism leads to *accelerated* rather than delayed tooth eruption—this contrasts sharply with rickets, cleidocranial dysplasia, and cherubism, which are known for delayed eruption patterns. In fact, congenital hyperthyroidism may even result in premature exfoliation of primary teeth due to increased metabolic activity affecting enamel formation and root resorption.
